points: Manufacturing is considered the backbone of economic development as it modernizes agriculture and creates jobs in secondary and tertiary sectors.
Industries are classified based on raw materials (Agro/Mineral), their role (Basic/Consumer), capital investment, ownership (Public/Private/Joint/Cooperative), and bulk/weight.
Agglomeration economies occur when industries cluster around urban centers to leverage services like banking, insurance, and transport.
Textile industries like cotton and jute are vital for employment and self-reliance, with cotton concentrated in the west and jute along the Hugli basin.
The sugar industry is seasonal and ideally suited for cooperatives, with a recent shift towards southern and western states due to higher sucrose content.
Basic industries like Iron and Steel support machinery for all other sectors, while rapidly growing segments include Chemicals, Fertilizers, Cement, and the Bengaluru-based IT sector.
Industries cause four major types of pollution—air, water, land, and noise—along with thermal pollution from hot effluents released into water bodies.
Treatment of industrial waste involves three phases: primary mechanical means, secondary biological processes, and tertiary chemical/physical recycling.
Sustainable development requires the integration of economic growth with environmental concerns, as demonstrated by the proactive management model of NTPC.
